author: @dougcox835
text: Speaking of capacitors... You should do a video on making your own. I think the best way would be pretty much how the real ones are made.  Find two spools of very thin foil (preferably a solderable metal) and a spool of some very thin plastic film.  The wind them all up together.  The trick is to make one foil slightly over the edge and the other foil slightly over the other edge so that in the final cylinder you have two "terminals" to attach to at either end.  I don't know anything about electrolytics though but I suppose you just soak everything in a solution.

text: Very exciting and interesting developments.  Has a lot of potential for a lot of different applications. replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: Rob did a recent vid on storing H2 in NH3, so those problems are far less. But, yes, Hydrogen can be a bit riskier than other flammable gasses as it has a much wider ignition/explosion range, from just a few % well into the 90%s; most others only over a 10-20% range.

author: @blackenedmerlin2076
text:
I don't see how cracking water then re-burning the hydrogen could ever be a net positive, but getting hydrogen from water by electrolysis loses a lot of energy.  Catalytic assistance could potentially reduce the losses and get closer to net zero.

There could also be issues of degradation of the paracatalysts as they are in fact base metals which can corrode away and not the true catalysts which remain stable in the reactions.  Perhaps the paracatalists will simply be cheap enough to be consumables.

Lastly, if ammonia allows stripping the hydrogen out easier than from water, perhaps our own waste streams (pun intended) could be feed stock to hydrogen production.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @simongross3122
replies.text: I don't think net positive energy is the goal.  I think we want something to burn that won't produce CO2.  If the energy we use to crack the H2 out of water or ammonia or methane, or the manufacture of those products, involves burning something that creates CO2 then we don't have clean energy.  I think the goal is to create H2 without using anything that creates CO2.  The cheaper the better.

author: @brainretardant
text: I have been doing a lot of thinking about how the magnet affects electrolysis and increases efficiency  at the anod/cathode as you demonstrated.  I think this capacitance effect is part of it.  The magnet orients spin and extends the life by localizing electron rich intermediates and not allowing them to migrate across the potential.  If you charge up a capacitor and disassemble it loses its charge, but on reassemble it regains.  Is the catalyst effect present only in the assembled I would presume but worth checking if in both

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text:
Decades ago I made a crude tiny air ioniser (capacitors/diodes ladder), I wondered how/if the coronal discharge would influence gas reactions and if it could drive a reaction in low pressure/concentrations without just triggering a 'bang', but I never pursued it. I wonder if a hv/hf coronal discharge would do any good in an IC engines exhaust to either replace or assist catalytic converters?   (a bit late now E-vehicles are coming  ;-/  ).
I did make an ozonizer with the previous circuit, so something must happen to gasses in such fields.   So... steam and HV field?

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text:
Well it might work to reduce the particulate emissions. These are (and probably will be for some time) a major problem in Diesel engines and to a lesser extent in petrol! 
It is a good concept!

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: @totherarf I didn't mean 'electrostatic-precipitation', I was thinking more of a coronal discharge sufficient to excite enough reaction to do what catalytic converters do; maybe enough hv/hf to fire a diffuse arc through at least part of the exhaust gasses and generate reactive species. Not as vicious as a 'plasma' arc, but diffuse yet still 'energetic'.

author: @keithianlocke
text:
The pharaohs extracted gold from sea salt by layering the salt between straw out in the sun, and moistened it with urine each day.
Gold can also be extracted from sea salt and rock salt using nitric acid...
Use the sun to extract salt from sea water. Make nitric acid using high voltage spark in enclosed space with air pumping through it to filter through water.
Then use the acid to extract gold from salt.

replies.author: @Barskor1

replies.text: I recently was asked if a solar panel can work from the campfire flames. So I got my phone sized panel and placed it in front of the glowing flame but the indicator led was very dim. When I got home I googled it and there is not enough of the right frequency to be effective unless you have a huge fire . So your idea of manipulating it  might make the difference in a camping  fire solar charger being possible !

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @Buzzhumma Maybe not Change the lowest freqency. Maybe instead it will change how much current flows or the voltage,  Maybe nothing interesting at all. I suppose it would be an interesting test.

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: The frequency absorbed by a photovoltaic cells is controlled by the band gap(s) of the semiconductor material. I hate to be the guy that says something is impossible, by all means check it out further, but that's a bit different from the alumina material used in this experiment.  According to the article: "the film is a defective semiconductor with an appreciable density of in-gap electronic states."  If you were to make a tunable solar panel, it would probably have to be made from scratch with such a similar material. Just my guess.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@DFPercush I just reviewed using an electric field in a vacuum tube to determin "stopping potential" of photo electrons.  The electric field doesn't effect the lowest freqency of light to make photo electrons. But an electric field can slow or speed up electrons in a vacuum tube depending on the field's direction.  The frequencies of photons that partake in photo electric effect In a semiconductor probably wont change with the application of an external electric field. but I wonder if it may effect efficiency or a cell's current and voltage. 

Apparently the PV cells develop an internal electric field at the NP junction.

Proposal to apply an external electric field to existing PV:  position a conductive screen some distance from the top plate. 
Apply an electric field to the screen  with a potential between the screen and the metal plate on the back of solar cell, then vary the electric  potential between screen and back plate.
